Lecture 8: Cross-Filling Properties of Projections ​

Topics: §5.2–5.3 — Cross-Filling Decomposition, VU=I Automatic, rank(P)=trace(P), Compatible Families Date: Apr 6 – Apr 9, 2026

Introduction ​

In Lecture 7, we introduced projection operators through the sunlight-floor model and proved key properties from the defining equation P2=P. We discovered that:

  • Col(P)={v:Pv=v} (vectors on the floor)
  • Null(P)={v:Pv=0} (vectors pointing to the sun)
  • Col(P)∩Null(P)={0} (floor and sunlight are not parallel)

But we still have two fundamental questions unanswered:

::: question Two Natural Questions

  1. How to construct projections? Given a floor and a sunlight direction, how do we write down the matrix P?

  2. What makes projections special? We know from Chapter 1 that any matrix can be written as A=UV via cross-filling. What happens when we cross-fill a projection? :::

This lecture answers the second question. We will discover that cross-filling projections reveals magical properties that do not hold for general matrices.

1. Cross-Filling a Projection ​

1.1 Review: Cross-Filling Any Matrix ​

Recall from Lecture 3 that any matrix A can be decomposed into a sum of rank-1 matrices:

A=R1+R2+⋯+Rr

where r=rank(A). We can collect these rank-1 pieces into a product form:

A=UV

where:

  • U is an m×r matrix whose columns are the "left parts" of each Ri
  • V is an r×n matrix whose rows are the "right parts" of each Ri

Example

Example 1.1: Cross-Filling a General Matrix

A=(2412)=(21)(12)=(21)(12)

Here U=(21) (one column) and V=(12) (one row).

We can verify: UV=(21)(12)=(2412)=A. ✓

But what about VU?

VU=(12)(21)=1⋅2+2⋅1=4≠I

For general matrices, VU is just some number or matrix — nothing special.

1.2 What Happens When We Cross-Fill a Projection? ​

Now let's try the same process with a projection matrix.

Example

Example 1.2: Cross-Filling a Projection

Consider the projection onto the x-axis:

P=(1000)

Verify it's a projection: P2=(1000)(1000)=(1000)=P. ✓

Cross-fill it:

P=(10)(10)

So U=(10) and V=(10).

Now compute VU:

VU=(10)(10)=1=I1

Remarkable! For this projection, VU=I.

Is this just a coincidence, or does it always happen?

Example

Example 1.3: Another Projection

Consider the projection onto the line y=x:

P=12(1111)

Verify: P2=12(1111)⋅12(1111)=14(2222)=12(1111)=P. ✓

Cross-fill it:

P=12(11)(11)

So U=12(11) and V=12(11) (we can scale to normalize).

Actually, let's just take U=(11) and V=12(11).

Then:

VU=12(11)(11)=12(1+1)=1=I1

Again, VU=I!

This pattern suggests a deep theorem.

2. The Fundamental Theorem: VU = I Automatic ​

2.1 Statement ​

Proposition

Theorem 2.1 (VU = I Automatic)

Let P be a projection (P2=P). Write P=UV as a cross-filling decomposition where:

  • U is m×r with r=rank(P)
  • V is r×n

Then VU=Ir automatically.

That is, V is a left inverse of U, and U is a right inverse of V.

Remark

Why Is This Remarkable?

For a general matrix A=UV, the product VU can be anything:

  • It might not even be square (if U and V have different numbers of columns/rows)
  • Even if square, it's typically not the identity

But for projections, P2=P forces VU=I automatically — no additional conditions needed!

2.2 Proof ​

The proof uses only the defining property P2=P and basic matrix algebra.

Given: P=UV and P2=P.

Step 1: Expand P2=P using P=UV:

UVUV=UV

Step 2: Left-cancel by U (meaning: left-multiply both sides by a left inverse of U).

Since U has full column rank (rank r), it has a left inverse. Applying it to both sides:

VUV=V

Step 3: Right-cancel by V (meaning: right-multiply both sides by a right inverse of V).

Since V has full row rank (rank r), it has a right inverse. Applying it to both sides:

VU=Ir

Done! ∎

Remark

Why Does U Have a Left Inverse?

U is m×r with rank r, meaning it has full column rank. This implies UTU is invertible, so U has a left inverse (UTU)−1UT.

Similarly, V has full row rank, so VVT is invertible and V has a right inverse VT(VVT)−1.

The proof above uses these facts implicitly.

3. Rank Equals Trace for Projections ​

The automatic property VU=I leads to a stunning consequence.

3.1 Trace of a Rank-1 Projection ​

First, let's compute the trace of a rank-1 projection.

Example

Example 3.1: Trace of a Rank-1 Projection

Consider P=(10)(10)=(1000).

This is a rank-1 projection. Its trace is:

trace(P)=1+0=1

Consider P=12(11)(11)=12(1111).

Its trace is:

trace(P)=12(1+1)=1

Proposition

Lemma 3.1 (Rank-1 Projection Has Trace 1)

If P=uvT is a rank-1 projection (P2=P), then:

trace(P)=1

Proof:

From VU=I (where U=u and V=vT are both 1×1 after reduction to rank-1 case), we have:

trace(P)=trace(UV)=trace(VU)=trace(I1)=1

∎

3.2 The Rank = Trace Theorem ​

Proposition

Theorem 3.2 (Rank = Trace for Projections)

For any projection P (satisfying P2=P):

rank(P)=trace(P)

Proof:

Cross-fill P into a sum of rank-1 projections:

P=R1+R2+⋯+Rr

where r=rank(P) and each Ri is a rank-1 matrix.

Key observation: Since P=UV with VU=I, we can write each Ri as a rank-1 projection:

Ri=UeieiTV

where ei is the i-th standard basis vector in Rr.

Each Ri satisfies:

Ri2=(UeieiTV)(UeieiTV)=UeieiT(VU)eieiTV=UeieiTIeieiTV=UeieiTV=Ri

So each Ri is a projection! By Lemma 3.1, trace(Ri)=1.

Therefore:

trace(P)=∑i=1rtrace(Ri)=∑i=1r1=r=rank(P)

∎

Remark

Why Doesn't This Work for General Matrices?

For a general matrix A=UV, we do NOT have VU=I, so the rank-1 pieces Ri=uiviT are NOT projections.

Without the projection property, we cannot conclude that trace(Ri)=1, and the whole argument breaks down.

This is why rank = trace is special to projections.

4. Compatible Families of Projections ​

Now we explore what happens when we have multiple projections that add up to the identity.

4.1 Definition ​

Definition

Compatible Family of Projections

A collection of projection matrices {P1,P2,…,Pk} (each satisfying Pi2=Pi) is called a compatible family if:

P1+P2+⋯+Pk=I

and

PiPj=0for all i≠j

Example

Example 4.1: Projections onto Coordinate Axes

In R3, consider:

P1=(100000000),P2=(000010000),P3=(000000001)

Check:

  • Each Pi2=Pi ✓
  • P1+P2+P3=I3 ✓
  • PiPj=0 for i≠j ✓

This is a compatible family.

4.2 Geometric Interpretation ​

Remark

What Does Compatibility Mean Geometrically?

If {P1,…,Pk} is a compatible family:

  1. P1+⋯+Pk=I means: every vector v can be uniquely decomposed as:

    v=P1v+P2v+⋯+Pkv

  2. PiPj=0 for i≠j means: the "floors" are mutually orthogonal in the sense that:

    Col(Pi)∩Col(Pj)={0}for i≠j

The space is decomposed into k subspaces that are "perpendicular" (in a generalized sense).

5. The Automatic Compatibility Theorem ​

Here comes the most surprising result of this lecture.

5.1 The Conjecture ​

Suppose we have projections P1,…,Pk (each Pi2=Pi), and we know:

P1+P2+⋯+Pk=P

where P is also a projection (P2=P).

Question: Does this automatically imply PiPj=0 for i≠j?

You might think: "This looks like a hard theorem to prove!" But using our cross-filling tools, the proof is almost trivial.

5.2 The Theorem ​

Proposition

Theorem 5.1 (Automatic Compatibility)

Let P1,…,Pk be projections (Pi2=Pi). If:

P1+P2+⋯+Pk=P

where P is also a projection (P2=P), then automatically:

PiPj=0for all i≠j

Remark

Why Is This Surprising?

The condition PiPj=0 seems completely independent from the condition P1+⋯+Pk=P.

But the projection property P2=P forces them to be orthogonal — no extra assumptions needed!

5.3 Proof Using Cross-Filling and Trace ​

Proof:

Cross-fill each Pi as Pi=UiVi. Since Pi2=Pi, we know ViUi=Iri where ri=rank(Pi).

Now collect all the columns of all Ui into one big matrix U, and stack all the rows of all Vi into one big matrix V:

P=P1+⋯+Pk=U1V1+⋯+UkVk=(U1U2⋯Uk)(V1V2⋮Vk)=UV

Step 1: Count the rank.

By the trace = rank theorem:

rank(P)=trace(P)=trace(P1)+⋯+trace(Pk)=rank(P1)+⋯+rank(Pk)

So:

rank(P)=r1+r2+⋯+rk

Step 2: Count the columns of U.

The matrix U=(U1U2⋯Uk) has exactly r1+r2+⋯+rk columns.

Since rank(P)=r1+⋯+rk, and Col(P)⊆Col(U), we must have:

rank(U)≥rank(P)=r1+⋯+rk

But U has exactly r1+⋯+rk columns, so:

rank(U)=r1+⋯+rk

This means U has full column rank!

Step 3: Conclude VU=I.

Since P=UV is a projection and U has full column rank, by Theorem 2.1:

VU=Ir1+⋯+rk

Step 4: Block structure implies orthogonality.

Write out VU in block form:

VU=(V1V2⋮Vk)(U1U2⋯Uk)=(V1U1V1U2⋯V1UkV2U1V2U2⋯V2Uk⋮⋮⋱⋮VkU1VkU2⋯VkUk)

Since VU=I, this must be a block diagonal matrix:

(Ir10⋯00Ir2⋯0⋮⋮⋱⋮00⋯Irk)

Therefore, ViUj=0 for all i≠j.

Step 5: Compute PiPj.

For i≠j:

PiPj=(UiVi)(UjVj)=Ui(ViUj)Vj=Ui⋅0⋅Vj=0

Done! ∎

Remark

The Magic of Cross-Filling

This proof would be much harder without the cross-filling perspective!

The key insight is:

  1. Trace = rank tells us how many columns U must have
  2. VU = I forces a block diagonal structure
  3. The block diagonal structure forces orthogonality

All of this flows naturally from P2=P. No additional conditions needed!

6. Summary and Looking Ahead ​

In this lecture, we discovered the magical properties of cross-filling projections:

TIP

Key Theorems

  1. VU = I Automatic (Theorem 2.1): If P=UV with P2=P, then VU=I.

  2. Rank = Trace (Theorem 3.2): For any projection P, rank(P)=trace(P).

  3. Automatic Compatibility (Theorem 5.1): If P1+⋯+Pk=P with each Pi2=Pi and P2=P, then PiPj=0 for i≠j.

These results show that projections have extremely rigid structure. The single equation P2=P imposes so many constraints that:

  • Cross-filling automatically produces left/right inverses
  • Rank equals trace
  • Sums of projections are automatically orthogonal

::: question What's Next?

We now understand the internal structure of projections (via cross-filling). But we still haven't answered our first question from the introduction:

How do we construct a projection from scratch?

Given:

  • A "floor" (a subspace W⊆Rn)
  • A "sunlight direction" (another subspace S⊆Rn)

How do we write down the matrix P that projects onto W along S?

This is the topic of Lecture 9: Constructing Projections. :::

Exercises ​

Problem

Exercise 8.1: Verify VU=I for the projection P=15(1224).

Exercise 8.2: Find two projections P1,P2 in R2 such that P1+P2=I and verify that P1P2=0.

Exercise 8.3: Show that if P is a projection, then trace(P)=dim⁡(Col(P)).

Exercise 8.4: Let P=(100010000) and Q=(000000001). Verify that {P,Q} is a compatible family.

Exercise 8.5: (Challenge) Suppose P1,P2,P3 are projections with P1+P2+P3=P where P2=P. Prove that P1,P2,P3 are linearly independent matrices (as elements of the vector space of n×n matrices).
